title: NG v. LM
text:
COMMONWEALTH OF THE BAHAMAS
IN THE COURT OF APPEAL
SCCivApp No. 211 of 2025
B E T W E E N
NG
Appellant
AND
LM
Respondent
BEFORE: The Honourable Mr. Justice Evans, P
The Honourable Madam Justice Charles, JA
The Honourable Mr. Justice Smith, JA
APPEARANCES: Mr. Robert Adams, KC, with Mr. Edward Marshall, II, and Ms.
Bryann Hepburn, Counsel for Intended Appellant
Mrs. Krystal Rolle, KC, with Mr. Darron Cash, Counsel for Intended
Respondent
DATES: 12 February; 21 April 2026
*******************************************
Civil Appeal – Discovery – Specific disclosure – Costs – Apportionment of costs – Exercise of
judicial discretion - Partial success - Rule 23 of the Matrimonial Causes Rules, Chapter 125
The Appellant (“the Husband”) filed a Summons pursuant to Rule 23 of Matrimonial Causes Rules,
Chapter 125, seeking specific disclosure of documents which was heard on 7 November 2024. On
22 January 2025, the learned judge refused the discovery sought in a written Ruling except for an
email the Respondent (“Wife”) was ordered to disclose.
On 14 May 2025, the learned judge ordered that the Wife recover 85% of her costs of the
application and that the Husband recover 15% of his costs, such costs to be taxed if not agreed. As
of the date of the submissions on costs, the Husband’s counsel stated that the Wife had still not
produced the documents ordered by the Court.
The Husband now appeals the costs order, challenging the propriety of the apportionment made
by the learned judge.
Held: Appeal dismissed. The Husband is to pay the costs of the appeal; such costs to be taxed if
not agreed.

It is clear that the learned Judge considered the Wife the successful party, but felt she should be
penalized for her unreasonable behavior. The result would have been the same whichever test the
Judge had elected to use, and as such, there is no basis for a finding that the learned Judge did not
exercise her discretion properly.
In considering this matter as a whole, it is clear that the Husband’s discovery application failed,
and that he could not be considered the successful party. This would be the case whether the test
being applied is the issue-based test or the principle of costs following the event espoused in Re
Elgindata Ltd. (No. 2).
In Re Elgindata Ltd. (No. 2) [1992] 1 W.L.R. 1207; applied.
____________________________________________________________________________
J U D G M E N T
____________________________________________________________________________
Judgment delivered by The Honourable Mr. Justice Evans, P:
INTRODUCTION
1. This is an appeal by the Appellant (“the Husband”) against the costs order made on 14 May
2025, following the determination of a discovery application. The Honourable Madam Justice
Hope Strachan (“the learned judge”) ordered that the Respondent (“the Wife”) recover 85% of
her costs of the application and that the Husband recover 15% of his costs, such costs to be
taxed if not agreed. The appeal challenges the propriety of that apportionment.
BACKGROUND
2. The application in the court below was made by the Husband by way of Summons filed on 21
October 2024 pursuant to Rule 23 of the Matrimonial Causes Rules, Chapter 125. The
Husband’s application was not successful, although he succeeded on one of the issues.
3. The Husband sought specific disclosure of certain documents from the Wife in relation to
allegations of abuse made towards her and the children of the family, A.D and G.G. The court
refused the application for discovery of the documents requested, except for the King’s College
email, which the court ruled that the Wife shall provide the email to the Husband.
4. At the end of the ruling, the court intended to award costs of the application to the Wife,
however, Counsel for the Husband objected. The objection was made on the basis that the
Husband was successful on one of the issues in dispute, in particular, the King’s College email,
and he should be awarded costs in respect of that issue. The Court, as a result, reserved its
decision regarding costs and granted leave to Counsel to make submissions within seven (7)
days. Both Counsel submitted written submissions to the court on 30 January 2025.
5. Counsel for the Wife submitted that the Wife had succeeded on the application and the
Husband had undoubtedly failed, and that there was, therefore, no basis for the Court to depart
2

from the usual rule that costs should follow the event and that such costs be awarded to the
Wife.
6. Counsel further contended that the application for discovery did not involve an argument on
the King’s College email and therefore, the Husband did not succeed on this application as the
Wife agreed to provide the documents, thus obviating the need for the Husband to advance the
application.
7. Counsel submitted that even if the King’s College emails were treated as a success, such
limited success in the context of the exceedingly wide nature of the requests was not sufficient
to deprive the Wife of the costs of the application, nor enable the Husband to recover costs
when the application has overwhelmingly failed.
8. Counsel for the Husband submitted that they sent a letter to Counsel for the Wife on 6 May
2024, inter alia, requesting copies of the correspondence between (i) the Wife and King’s
College and (ii) the Wife and Dr. Kerr which contained allegations of abuse against the
Husband by the Wife against the minor children of the family and herself.
9. The Wife chose not to respond to the Husband’s letter requesting documents and remained
silent while addressing other matters in this and other related actions. Consequently, some five
(5) months after the request for the documents was made, the Husband filed the Summons
seeking an order directing the Wife to produce, by way of specific disclosure, copies of the
documents he had requested as well as copies of other documents he reasonably believed to be
in the Wife’s possession, custody and power based on references made to such documents in
other correspondence he already possessed.
10. The Summons was heard by the learned judge on 7 November 2024. During that hearing, and
only after Counsel for the Husband had completed his oral submissions in support of the
summons, in reply, Counsel for the Wife conceded that the correspondence exchanged between
the Wife and King’s College containing allegations of abuse against the Husband was relevant
to the Husband’s allegations against the Wife and ought to be disclosed to him on that basis.
11. On 22 January 2025, the trial judge delivered her written Ruling to the parties, dismissing all
of the requests for discovery save the correspondence between the Wife and King’s College.
The Wife was directed to disclose the correspondence between her and King’s College to the
Husband, with the costs of and occasioned by the Summons to be determined.
12. As of the date of the submissions on costs, according to the Husband’s counsel, the Wife had
still not produced for the Husband’s inspection any of the documents the Wife had been ordered
to produce by the Court.
THE COURT’S FINDING
13. The essence of the learned judge’s findings is seen from paragraphs 25 to 29 as follows:
3

“[25.] The Court also considered the Court of Appeal decision
of Rawson McDonald v Paul R. Major SCCivApp No. 94 of
2017. Crane-Scott, JA made reference to the authority of
Scherer and another v. Counting instruments Ltd and another
[1986] 2 All ER 529 which Buckley LJ at page 537 derived ten
(10) guiding principles in relation to costs. The Court said:
“From the cases which we have cited and from Ottway v
Jones [1955] 2 All ER 585, [1955]1 1 WLR 706, Baylis
Baxter Ltd v Sabath [1958] 2 All ER 209, [1958] 1 WLR
529 and William C Parker Ltd v F J Ham & Son Ltd
[1972] 3 AIl ER 1051, [1972] 1 WLR 1583, which were
also referred to by counsel, we derive the following
propositions. (1) The normal rule is that costs follow the
event. That party who turns out to have unjustifiably
either brought another party before the court or given
another party cause to have recourse to the court to
obtain his rights is required to recompense that other
party in costs. But, (2) the judge has under s 50 of the
1925 Act an unlimited discretion to make what order as
to costs he considers that the justice of the case requires.
(3) Consequently, a successful party has a reasonable
expectation of obtaining an order for his costs to be paid
by the opposing party but has no right to such an order,
for it depends on the exercise of the court's discretion. (4)
This discretion is not one to be exercised arbitrarily: it
must be exercised judicially, that is to say in accordance
with established principles and in relation to the facts of
the case. (5) The discretion cannot be well exercised
unless there are relevant grounds for its exercise, for its
exercise without grounds cannot be a proper exercise of
the judge's function. (6) The grounds must be connected
with the case. This may extend to any matter relating to
the litigation and the parties’ conduct in it, and also to
the circumstances leading to the litigation, but no further.
(7) If no such ground exists for departing from the
normal rule, or if, although such grounds exist, the judge
is known to have acted not on any such ground but on
some extraneous ground, there has effectively been no
exercise of the discretion. (8) If a party invokes the
jurisdiction of the court to grant him some discretionary
relief and establishes the basic grounds therefor but the
relief sought is denied in the exercise of the discretion...
the opposing party may properly be ordered to pay his
costs. But where the party who invokes the court's
jurisdiction wholly fails to establish one or more of the
4

ingredients necessary to entitle him to the relief claimed,
whether discretionary or not, it is difficult to envisage a
ground on which the opposing party could properly be
ordered to pay his costs... (9) If a judge, having relevant
grounds on which to do so, has on those grounds, or some
of them, made an order as to costs in the exercise of his
discretion, his decision is final unless he gives leave to a
dissatisfied party to appeal. (10) If, however, he has made
his order having no relevant grounds available or having
in fact acted on extraneous grounds, this court can
entertain an appeal without leave and can make what
order it thinks fit.” [Emphasis added]
…
[26.] In considering the principles in Scherer v Counting, supra,
the Court accepts that the husband requested the email to
King’s College from the wife by a letter dated 6 May 2024. The
wife did not provide the email. Subsequently, the husband filed
the Summons dated 21 October 2024 for discovery. The Court
agrees with Counsel for the husband that it was only when
Counsel completed his oral submissions that Counsel for the
wife in her submissions agreed to provide the King’s College
email. Paragraph 22 of the ruling in relation to this point is
outlined below:
“22. In relation to the King's College email, there is a
reference in the Affidavit of the Applicant to
communication between the Respondent and King’s
College. Therefore, there is a basis to suspect that there
is communication that an email exists and it is not in the
Applicant’s possession. In the premises the necessary
foundation has been met in the Affidavit of the Applicant
as to the existence and relevance of this email to King's
College. Counsel for the Respondent has agreed to
provide this email to Counsel for the Applicant.”
[27.] In these circumstances, the wife’s failure to provide the
King’s College email that was in her possession to the husband
may have resulted in the application for discovery being made.
Notwithstanding the concession made by Counsel for the wife,
the Court cannot disregard the time and resources used by
Counsel for the husband to file his application. Whilst Counsel
was not successful on every issue, he did succeed on the King’s
College issue and as such he should be awarded costs in that
5

regard. The wife, however was successful on all other issues and
should be awarded her costs of these proceedings.
[28.] It has been brought to the Court’s attention, through the
husband’s submissions, that following the ruling that was
delivered in respect of this matter, the wife has not provided the
King’s College email to the husband. The Court’s ruling is not
to be ignored. The email is to be provided to the husband within
seven (7) days of this ruling.
CONCLUSION
[29.] In exercising my discretion, the court orders:
1. That the wife having succeeded in defending the
application for discovery of the husband on all of the
issues, except for disclosure of the document from King’s
College shall have Eighty-Five (85) % of her costs to be
taxed if not agreed.
2. That the husband having succeeded on the application
for discovery of the King’s College document shall have
Fifteen (15) % of his costs to be taxed if not agreed.”
LEAVE TO APPEAL FROM THE COURT BELOW
14. On 21 May 2025, the Husband filed his application before the learned judge for leave to appeal
the Ruling on costs in accordance with Rule 27(5) of the Court of Appeal Rules. He was granted
leave to appeal the Ruling on costs on 20 November 2025. The learned judge therefore took
about six (6) months to make her decision on that application for leave to appeal. It was
therefore necessary for the Husband to make an application for an extension of time in which
to file her appeal before this Court.
15. On 24 November 2025, a mere four (4) days after leave to appeal was granted, the Husband
filed his Notice of Appeal in this Court (albeit without leave to extend the time for appealing)
and served the same upon the Wife by their Counsel.
APPLICATION FOR EXTENSION OF TIME WITHIN WHICH TO FILE AN APPEAL
16. It was not until 19 January 2026 that the Husband made an application for an extension of the
time allotted to file his appeal. The application was supported by an Affidavit of the Husband
filed on the same date.
17. The application for the extension came on for hearing before us on 12 February 2026. At that
hearing, the extension of time was granted by agreement, and there was no order as to costs.
6

We ordered the parties to provide written submissions, and we indicated that we would deal
with the matter on the papers.
18. The Husband filed his submissions on 5 March 2026, and the Wife filed hers on 18 March
2026. However, also filed with the Wife’s submission was a Notice of Preliminary Objection
accompanied by an Affidavit sworn by Wallace I Rolle on behalf of the Wife. In his Affidavit,
Mr. Rolle asserted that the Notice of Motion filed by the Husband on 16 February 2026 was
inconsistent with the leave granted by the court below, as well as that sanctioned by this Court
in granting the extension of time.
19. Specifically, at paragraph 13 of his Affidavit, Mr. Rolle stated as follows:
“13. Specifically, the Draft Notice of Appeal attached to the
Appellant’s Summons seeking leave and exhibited to the
Affidavit seeking an extension of time at Paragraph (i) on Page
3 states, “...FOR AN ORDER: (i) Setting aside the Ruling on
Costs delivered by the Learned Judge in its entirety, or in
alternative, varied” while the Notice of Appeal filed by the
Appellant on 16th February, 2026 pursuant to such leave and
extension of time at Paragraph (i) on Page 3 states “FOR AN
ORDER: Setting aside the Ruling on Costs delivered by the
Learned Judge in its entirety and remitting back to the Learned
Judge for re-hearing, or in alternative, varied by setting aside
the order that the Appellant pay costs to the Respondent and
directing the Respondent to pay half of the Appellant's costs in
respect of the application before the Learned Judge in the court
below”; [Emphasis Added]” “[Emphasis Added]
20. We have received no response from the Husband relative to the Rolle Affidavit. However, it is
clear that the Notice filed on 16 February 2026 is inconsistent with the Notice approved by this
Court in granting the extension. At the hearing before us Mr. Adams, KC did indicate an
intention to expand on his submissions, but no leave was sought nor granted to alter or amend
his Notice of Appeal.
21. As a result, on the facts before us, we feel constrained to consider the appeal on the terms of
the Draft Notice exhibited to his application for the extension of time. I do not see that this
affects our ability to deal with this matter because if necessary, we are able to set aside or vary
the learned judge’s order and give any directions or make such order which we consider will
meet the justice of the case. In my view, the Wife is in no way prejudiced as the actual grounds
of appeal remain the same.
THE APPEAL
22. The grounds of appeal set out in the Notice of Motion are as follows:
7

“1. The Learned Judge erred in law and failed to apply the well
settled principles governing the exercise of the court’s discretion
respecting the award of costs in relation to a partially successful
party as determined and applied in Elgindata Ltd. (No. 2) [1992]
1 W.L.R. 1207 per Nourse J; namely, that (a) the general rule
regarding the award of costs does not cease to apply because the
successful party raises issues or makes allegations on which he
has failed and (b) a court ought only deprive a partially
successful party of its costs and/or order that party to pay the
whole or a part of the other party’s costs in circumstances where
the court has found the partially successful party raised issues
or made allegations ‘improperly’ or ‘unreasonably’.
2. The Learned Judge erred in law when she awarded eighty five
percent (85%) of the Respondent’s costs to be taxed if not agreed
in circumstances where no finding was made in the Ruling on
Costs that the issues raised by the Appellant in the Discovery
Application or the allegations made by him therein were either
improper or unreasonable.
3. The Learned Judge also erred in law when she awarded
fifteen percent (15%) of the Appellant's costs to be taxed if not
agreed based on her determination in paragraph 27 of the
Ruling on Costs that the court is unable to disregard the time
and resources used by Counsel for the Appellant to file the
Discovery Application in circumstances where it was also
determined in paragraph 26 of the Ruling on Costs that the
Discovery Application was necessitated by the Respondent’s
refusal to provide material correspondence she was obligated to
provide to the Appellant despite requests having been made for
that correspondence since 6 May 2024 and had only agreed to
provide the Kings College correspondence at the hearing of the
Discovery Application.
4. The Appellant hereby reserves the right to supplement or add
additional grounds of appeal to this Notice of Appeal.
SUBMISSIONS, ANALYSIS, AND FINDINGS
23. In my opinion, the grounds of appeal raise the sole issue of whether the learned judge erred in
principle, or otherwise exceeded the ambit of her discretion in apportioning the costs of the
discovery application as she did.
24. Mr. Adams, KC contended that the learned Judge erred in treating the matter as one appropriate
for an issue-based apportionment. He asserted that she was required to have recourse to the
8

Matrimonial Causes Rules, specifically Rule 62(3), which he contends governed the Learned
Judge’s decision on costs at that time.
25. He stated further that Rule 62 (3) of the Matrimonial Causes Rules provides that the costs
allowed to attorneys and taxation of such costs, shall be in accordance with the Rules of the
Supreme Court of England in force at the time, so far as the same are applicable. The Rules of
the Supreme Court of England in force in 1957 were the Annual Practice 1957 (“the 1957
Rules”). This position remained until 1 July 2025, when the Matrimonial Causes Rules were
amended and the Matrimonial Causes (Amendment) Rules 2025 came into effect. It was at that
time that the provisions of The Supreme Court Civil Procedure Rules, 2022 (“the CPR”)
became the default rules for all matrimonial causes, including the present one.
26. As a consequence, he concluded that it is manifest that when the Ruling on Costs was made
on 15 May 2025, the Learned Judge could not rely upon the costs procedure outlined in the
CPR and was bound to follow the costs procedure outlined in Rule 62(3) of the Matrimonial
Causes Rules.
27. The issue based test in the award of costs operates on the principle that costs should be
allocated between parties based on specific issues in the particular proceedings. As such where
a party has won overall on the case but has lost on one or more issues, the successful party is
entitled to his costs unless the Court is satisfied that the costs were materially increased by his
unreasonably raising certain issues.
28. Counsel further submitted that the principle in the case of In Re Elgindata Ltd. (No. 2) [1992]
1 WLR 1207 is what should have guided the Learned Judge. In the Elgindata case, the position
was explained by the English Court of Appeal as follows:
“I now come to Warner J's judgment on costs. Having referred
to the general rule that a successful plaintiff is entitled to his
costs notwithstanding that he may have failed on some issues, he
said that he had to look at the realities of the case.
…
In order to show that the judge erred I must state the principles
which ought to have been applied. They are mainly recognized
or provided for, it matters not which, by section 51 of the
Supreme Court Act 1981 and the relevant provisions of R.S.C.,
Ord. 62, in this case rules 2(4), 3(3) and 10. They do not in their
entirety depend on the express recognition or provision of the
rules. In part they depend on established practice or implication
from the rules. The principles are these. (i) Costs are in the
discretion of the court. (ii) They should follow the event, except
when it appears to the court that in the circumstances of the case
some other order should be made. (iii) The general rule does not
cease to apply simply because the successful party raises issues
9

or makes allegations on which he fails, but where that has caused
a significant increase in the length or cost of the proceedings he
may be deprived of the whole or a part of his costs. (iv) Where
the successful party raises issues or makes allegations
improperly or unreasonably, the court may not only deprive him
of his costs but may order him to pay the whole or a part of the
unsuccessful party's costs. Of these principles the first, second
and fourth are expressly recognized or provided for by rules
2(4), 3(3) and 10 respectively. The third depends on well-
established practice. Moreover, the fourth implies that a
successful party who neither improperly nor unreasonably
raises issues or makes allegations on which he fails ought not to
be ordered to pay any part of the unsuccessful party’s costs. It
was because of his disregard of that principle that the judge
erred in this case.” [Emphasis Added]
29. Mr. Adams contended that in this case, there is no finding in the Ruling on Costs that the
Husband misconducted himself in any way by making the discovery application, justifying the
departure from the general principle.
30. Mrs. Rolle, KC on behalf of the Wife, submitted that the issue as to the CPR does not arise
from any of the grounds in the Notice of Motion. However she further contends that having
regard to the fact that the Husband’s application for discovery related to a number of items
and only one was ordered by agreement, there is no reasonable basis on which it could be said
that the Husband was the successful party. She further noted that in any event he received costs
relative to his success on that one item. In these circumstances she concluded that there was
no basis to depart from the principle that costs follow the event.
31. In considering this matter as a whole, it is clear that the Husband’s discovery application failed
and in my view, he could not be considered the successful party. This would be the case whether
the test being applied is the issue-based test or the principle of costs following the event
espoused in Elgindata.
32. The Learned Judge was clear on the basis on which she awarded to the Husband 15% costs.
This is seen at paragraph 27 of her Judgment, where she stated as follows:
“27… the wife’s failure to provide the King’s College email that
was in her possession to the husband may have resulted in the
application for discovery being made. Notwithstanding the
concession made by Counsel for the wife, the Court cannot
disregard the time and resources used by Counsel for the
husband to file his application. Whilst Counsel was not
successful on every issue, he did succeed on the King’s College
issue and as such he should be awarded costs in that regard. The
wife, however was successful on all other issues and should be
awarded her costs of these proceedings.”
10

33. It is clear that the learned judge considered the Wife the successful party but felt she should be
penalized for her unreasonable behavior. In my view, the result would have been the same
whichever test the learned judge had elected to use. The Husband has failed to demonstrate
any basis upon which this Court could properly interfere with the learned judge’s exercise of
her discretion.
CONCLUSION
34. For the foregoing reasons, I would dismiss the appeal and order that the Husband pay the costs
of the appeal; such costs to be taxed if not agreed.
__________________________________________
The Honourable Mr. Justice Evans, P
35. I agree.
__________________________________________
The Honourable Madam Justice Charles, JA
36. I also agree.
__________________________________________
The Honourable Mr. Justice Smith, JA

title: Albert Higgs v. Leshelmaryas Investment Company Limited
text:
COMMONWEALTH OF THE BAHAMAS
IN THE COURT OF APPEAL
SCCivApp. No. 212 of 2024
IN THE MATTER of ALL THAT piece parcel or tract of land situate on the Southwestern Side
of the New Harold Road Reservation in the Western District of the Island of New Providence
aforesaid comprising Ninety-two and Thirty-three Hundredths (92.33) acres more or less and
bounded Northwardly by land the property of the Bahamas Government Northeastwardly by the
New Harold Road Reservation leading to John F. Kennedy Drive Eastwardly partly by land
property of the Air Ministry partly by other land the property of Nassauvian Limited partly by land
formerly leased to the late Hedley Edwards and partly by other land and partly by land reserved
for the Bahamas Geodetic Survey southwardly by Crown Land Westwardly partly by land now or
formerly the property of The Caves Company Limited and partly by land now or formerly the
property of G. A. And S. G. Bostfield which said piece parcel or tract of land has such position
shape mars and dimensions as are shown on the diagram or plan attached to a Certificate of Title
issued by the Supreme Court of the Bahama Islands to Nassauvian Limited on the 23rd day of
February, A.D. 1970 and recorded in the Registry of Records in the City of Nassau in the said
Island of New Providence in Volume 1582 at pages 182 to 185 and is delineated on that part which
is coloured pink on the said diagram or plan.
AND IN THE MATTER of the Partition Act, Chapter 153
B E T W E E N
ALBERT HIGGS on behalf of the Estate of Kenneth McKinney Higgs Sr., Deceased
ALBERT HIGGS on behalf of the Estate of Clothilda Eugenia Higgs, Deceased
Appellants
AND
LESHELMARYAS INVESTMENT COMPANY LIMITED
Respondent
BEFORE: The Honourable Madam Justice Charles, JA
The Honourable Mr. Justice Smith, JA
The Honourable Madam Justice Fraser, JA
APPEARANCES: Ms. Bridget Francis-Butler, Counsel for the Appellants
Mr. Damian Gomez, KC, with Mrs. Lilnique Murphy-Grant, Counsel
for the Respondent
Mr. Timothy Eneas, KC, with Ms. Ava La-Roda, appeared, holding a
watching brief
DATES: 25 September 2025; 22 January 2026; 21 April 2026
*****************************************************

Civil Appeal – Partition of land — Consent to partition — Affidavit evidence — No cross-
examination — Weight of Evidence – Appellate interference with exercise of discretion – Valuation
of parcels — Use and enjoyment of land
The parties, after a long and convoluted litigation history, consented to a partition of the land.
Neither party sought a sale, and they agreed to create a 60-foot road reservation at the northern
boundary, leaving the issue of partition to be determined by the court on affidavit evidence,
including expert reports, without cross-examination.
The matter proceeded before the trial judge on competing affidavit evidence. The trial judge,
exercising his discretion, ordered a partition in accordance with the parties’ entitlement, allocating
separate parcels subject to a road reservation and easement, and directing that a portion of the
Appellants’ land be set aside for a judgment creditor of the Appellant.
The Appellants appealed that decision, contending, namely, that the trial judge erred in his
assessment of the value of the respective parcels and in the resulting partition, including his
findings on value, reliance on use and enjoyment, treatment of valuation factors, reliance on a prior
decision which was set aside, and that the decision was against the weight of the evidence.
Held: The appeal is dismissed. The partition is affirmed. The Appellants are to pay the
Respondent’s costs of the appeal, to be taxed in default of agreement.
The Court found that there was no mandate to partition by reference to the valuation of the
respective shareholdings, and that given the constraints with regard to the evidence presented, this
exercise of discretion by the trial judge was the best that could be done in the circumstances.
The Court also found that the trial judge’s conclusions that: (1) road access would remediate any
disparity in value, and (2) the decades of usage of the land by the Appellants were relevant factors
and were not plainly wrong and ought not to be set aside on appeal.
The Court also concluded that it would be an abuse of process to remit the matter back to the trial
court for reconsideration. As the successful party, the Respondent was awarded its costs of the
appeal.
Bahamasair Holdings Ltd. v. Messier Dowty Inc [2018] UKPC 25, applied
Campbell v. Campbell [2017] EWHC 182, mentioned
Higgs Sr. v. Leshel Maryas Investment Company Limited & Annamae Woodside [2009] UKPC 47,
mentioned
Super Value Food Stores Ltd. v. Yvonne Rahming SCCivApp No. 256 of 2017, applied
2

_____________________________________________________________________________
J U D G M E N T
_____________________________________________________________________________
Judgment delivered by The Honourable Mr. Justice Smith, JA:
INTRODUCTION
1. The subject matter of this appeal is a parcel of land comprising approximately 92.33 acres in
Western New Providence in the vicinity of Tonique Williams Darling Highway.
2. After a long and convoluted litigation history dating back to 1967, the dispute had resolved
itself to a determination of a partition as between the Appellants (“Higgs”) and the
Respondent. Neither party wanted a sale of the land. They consented to the court making an
order for partition.
3. The parties put forward their evidence in affidavit form. These Affidavits included 3 expert
reports. There was no cross-examination. The parties left the decision on the partitioning of
the land to the discretion of, Winder C.J. (“the trial judge”).
4. The trial judge decided to partition the land in accordance with the parties’ entitlement,
namely ¼ to the Respondent and ¾ to the Appellants. The division was as per the plan attached
(see exhibit A at page 11 of this Judgment). Parcel A to the Higgs and Parcel B to the
Respondent, save for a road reservation over which the parties would have an easement.
Further, by agreement of the Appellants, a judgment creditor of the Appellants, one Neely’s
of Nassau (Neely’s), was to have a 9 acre parcel of their land partitioned out of the Northern
portion of their Parcel A.
5. The trial judge also ordered each party to bear their own costs.
6. The Appellants have appealed that decision.
7. For the reasons that appear in this Judgment, I would dismiss this appeal and order the
Appellants to pay the Respondent’s costs of the appeal. Such costs to be taxed by the Registrar
in default of agreement.
THE DECISION OF THE TRIAL JUDGE
8. As background to the Decision of the trial judge, the following four factors should be noted.
9. First, the parties had agreed to create a 60 foot road reservation at the northern boundary of
the property where it meets Tonique Williams Darling Highway. This would ensure the
highest value to each parcel of land.
10. Second, the parties agreed to let the matter be decided on the opposing Affidavits of the parties
and other uncontested evidence. There was no cross examination.
3

11. Third, while the Decision of the trial judge at paragraph 37 of his Decision states that the
property was to be partitioned as follows: “(1) The portion of the Property identified as
Parcel A or 22.55 acres to the Higgs.” This is an obvious error as Parcel A as agreed was
67.78 acres and Parcel B was 22.55 acres. Parcel A was to devolve upon the Appellants
(Higgs) and Parcel B to the Respondent.
12. Fourth, the division of the land was in accordance with a prior order made by Thompson J. in
2006. This order had been set aside on appeal to the Court of Appeal and the Privy Council.
However, the basis upon which the prior order of Thompson J. had been set aside was that it
was premature, in that it required proper evidence upon an application for partition. The
question of partition was therefore referred back to the Supreme Court of the Bahamas.
Interestingly enough, the Privy Council was of the opinion that this might have been a fit case
for the exercise of the power of sale rather than partition (see paragraphs 59 and 60 of the
decision in Higgs Sr. v. Leshel Maryas Investment Company Limited & Annamae
Woodside [2009] UKPC 47). However, the matter had finally resolved itself to an acceptance
of the remaining parties for a partition of the land and not a sale.
13. The hearing of that partition application was conducted by the trial judge solely upon
competing affidavits and other uncontested evidence.
14. The trial judge summarized the respective cases put forward by the parties at paragraphs 9 to
22 of his Decision.
15. The case of the Respondent was basically that it had developed Parcel B and erected a
commercial facility that provided entertainment to the general public in the form of a bowling
alley and other related activities. The Appellants in no way contributed to this. On the
contrary, the Respondent alleged that the Appellants had mined Parcel A over the decades of
their possession. They have thus benefited from the land to the exclusion of the Respondent
and it would be unfair to ignore this in the partitioning of the land.
16. The case for the Appellants was basically that it was the Respondent who extensively mined
Parcel B and has benefited from the occupation of the more valuable portion of land. They
asked the court below:
“… to have regard to the use and enjoyment of the land, the
interest of the parties, its valuation, development and potential
future use … and in light of the valuation in respect to the
acreage occupied by [the Respondent], when compared to the
valuation of the remaining portion of the Property occupied by
the Higgs, it is proposed that [the Respondent’s] ¼ interest in
the land ought not to include more than 13 acres … ” (See
paragraph 18 of the trial judge’s Decision)
4

17. The trial judge then analysed the expert reports provided by the parties at paragraphs 28 to 33
of the Decision. One report was provided by each of the parties and another from a former
defendant to the action, one Annamae Woodside.
18. The trial judge then concluded at paragraph 34 as follows:
“Having given careful and anxious consideration to all of the
attendant circumstances of this case, I am not satisfied that I
should make any division other than the natural one made by
Thompson J. in 2006 giving the ¼ or 22.55 acre Parcel B to
Leshel. The remaining ¾ or 67.78 acre Parcel A ought therefore
to now be granted the Higgs.”
19. As to the differences in the Appellants’ suggested valuation of the parcels, the trial judge was
of the view that this was because of the road access and proximity to commercial activity to
Parcel B. This, he opined, could be remediated by road access. I quote paragraph 35 of the
Decision:
“On the evidence there is a considerable ridge remaining on
Parcel A from which the Higgs may continue their quarrying
activities. I therefore found little merit in the suggestion that the
land in Parcel B is of greater value per acre that (sic) Parcel A.
This could only be as a result of the road access and the
proximity to the commercial activity of the Bowling Alley. This
could be remediated by road access.”
20. Further, the trial judge opined that the Appellants too had significantly benefited from their
long use of the property. This was a further reason to stay with the partition as suggested by
the prior order of Thompson J. in 2006. The trial judge stated at paragraph 36 of the Decision:
“I accepted the assessment of Mr. McKinney KC, for Leshel,
that ‘the Higgs have little if any entitlement to raise the issue of
valuation, having regard to the fact that from 1967 and before,
when the first action started and continuing on to now, they have
been on the property, having been making a living off the
property’. I did not accept the suggestion by the Higgs that their
prior excavation took place at a time when they were the sole
owners of the property and should otherwise not be considered.
As this matter has been in the Court system since the 1970’s
there is little merit in this submission. The interest of
Nassauvian, the predecessor of Leshel, has existed since the
1970’s. Likewise, against this background, I did not accept that
the disputed complaint that Leshel may have violated the
injunction ought to change the outcome.”
5

21. The trial judge therefore ordered that the property be partitioned giving Parcel A to the
Appellants and Parcel B to the Respondent. Further, the trial judge ordered that 9 acres of the
Northern section of Parcel A be partitioned and given to Neely’s (a judgment creditor of the
Appellants).
THE APPEAL
22. The Appellants have listed 7 grounds of appeal as follows:
“1. The Learned Judge misdirected himself in fact and in law as
it relates to the physical value of the respective parcels of land
and therefore erred in awarding to the Respondent Parcel B,
excluding the road reservation at Tonique Williams Darling
Highway.
2. The learned judge failed to make specific findings of fact as to
the value of each share of the parcel land. The court merely
determined a value of one quarter (1/4) interest of the total
acreage. The court’s findings were therefore insufficient to
support the conclusion that each share of each parcel would be
materially equal in share of the whole parcel. The physical
division or partition of the land requires the physical inspection,
measurements calculations and considerations to determine the
specific value of the respective shares.
3. The Learned Judge erred in fact and law by relying on the use
of the land in determining whether substantial injury would
result to the parties by the actual partition of the land.
Substantial injury to a party does not occur simply because an
actual partition would not result in the highest and best use of
the land, if the land is partitioned without taking into account
its value. The Learned Judge failed to distinguish between the
value of the respective shares and the respective entitlement of
the parties.
4. The learned Judge misdirected himself when he concluded
that the increase value of the Parcel B was solely as a result of
the development by the Respondent and opined that the
Appellant ought not to benefit from the said development of the
Respondent. The increased value of the land was based
substantially upon the surrounding developments within the
area including the development of subdivision (High Point), the
implementation and construction of roads and utilities which
are not by the Respondent nor the Appellant.
6

5. That in all the circumstances the learned judge failed to take
into account all the evidence that negatively affected the value
of the land which was apportioned to the Appellant. The
Learned Judge failed to consider the depreciation of the value
of the property due to the emission of raw sewerage by a third
party onto the Southern boundary of the subject property. The
raw sewerage is a hazard to the environment and has damaged
the said land thereby effecting the use and enjoyment of a
substantial portion of the said land.
6. The Learned Judge erred in upholding the 2006 decision of
Thompson J. as she then was, which was subsequently appealed
to the Court of Appeal and set aside, and confirmed by the
Council of the Privy Council.
7. That in all the circumstances the judgment is against the
weight of the evidence.”
23. Having examined the written submissions and after entertaining oral submissions, this appeal
really raises 3 issues for consideration.
24. Grounds 1, 2, 3, 4 and 7 contend that the trial judge was wrong not to use their suggested
valuation of the land in deciding on a partition. In that case, as suggested at paragraph 16
above, the Respondent would only have been entitled to about 13 acres of the land.
25. At Ground 5, the Appellants contend that the trial judge failed to consider the depreciation in
value of the land caused by the Respondent permitting the emission of raw sewerage onto the
land.
26. At Ground 6, the Appellants contend that it was wrong for the trial judge to follow the
suggested partition of Thompson J. in 2006 since this had been set aside by the Court of
Appeal and the Privy Council.
ANALYSIS
Grounds 1,2,3,4 and 7
27. Both parties to this appeal agreed that in deciding on a partition of land, the courts have a
broad and unfettered discretion to apportion the land in a manner that was fair and just in the
circumstances of the case in question.
28. In spite of this, the Appellants sought to contend that the guiding factor in this action should
be an apportionment by value of the respective shareholdings.
29. Although authorities were cited by the Appellants to support this contention, no specific
references were provided for any part of the authorities. When pressed to do so in oral
argument, counsel for the Appellants could not point to any specific authority for their
contention that valuation was the cornerstone for any partition. In fact, while counsel cited an
7

authority which, she alleged, supported her contention, namely Campbell v. Campbell
[2017] EWHC 182, the allegedly relevant portion from paragraph 154 of the judgment was
an insertion of 3 pages from an unidentified and unverified source into the case. Counsel could
proffer no explanation for this misquoted and unverified addition to the case.
30. In the end, the Appellant accepted that the court did have a general and unfettered discretion
to partition in accordance with what was fair and just in all the circumstances. There was no
mandate to partition by reference to the valuation of the respective shareholdings (see
generally Halsbury’s Laws of England 1st ed. vol. 21 at paragraph 1605).
31. In written submissions, Counsel for the Appellants contend that:
“3.1 The apportionment of value in a Partition action should
align with the respective beneficial interest of the co-owners,
unless there are overriding considerations or agreements that
dictate otherwise. Thus, it would not generally be equitable or
justifiable for the title owner with ¼ interest to be apportioned
a greater value than the party with a ¾ interest in the land. In
the present case Leshel was granted 22.55 acres at $300,000.00
per acre with a face value in excess of $6,765,000.00 whereas the
Higgs who are entitled to ¾ interest in the subject property was
granted Parcel B with approximately 67 acres valued at
$50,000.00 to $100,000.00 per acre at greatest value of
$6,700.00.00 (sic) provided we accept the 2010 appraised value
of Robin Brownrigg of Bahamas Realty.”
32. According to Counsel, this was not fair and just.
33. However, this argument was based upon their untested valuation report, and as the trial judge
observed, this deficiency in valuation “could be remediated by road access” which both
parties agreed to create. Further, the Appellants had benefited over decades from mining
activities on the land and this too would compensate for any perceived valuation differences.
34. As Evans J.A. (as he then was) observed in the case Super Value Food Stores Ltd. and
anor. v. Yvonne Rahming SCCivApp No. 256 of 2017 at paragraph 30:
“It is well established in law that an appellate court will not
interfere with the exercise of a Judge’s discretion unless it is
clearly satisfied that the discretion has been exercised on a
wrong principle and should have been exercised in a contrary
way or that there has been a miscarriage of justice. …”
35. It is my humble view, that given the constraints with regard to the evidence presented, this
exercise of discretion by the trial judge was the best that could be done in the circumstances.
I see no error of principle nor other fault in the exercise of his discretion. Alternatively, the
trial judge’s exercise of discretion was not plainly wrong and ought not to be set aside on
8

appeal (see generally Bahamasair Holdings Ltd. v. Messier Dowty Inc [2018] UKPC 25 at
paragraph 33).
Ground 5
36. This ground alleges that the trial judge failed to consider the acts of waste committed by the
Respondent. Specifically, that the Respondent allowed raw sewage onto the property and thus
caused a depreciation in the value of the land. This, they contend, should have affected his
appointment.
37. This ground is without merit.
38. At paragraph 33 of the Decision, the trial judge devoted 2 ½ pages of consideration to the
valuation report provided by the former defendant, Annamae Woodside. In this analysis the
trial judge detailed all the alleged acts of waste committed on the property and he noted the
conclusion that:
“[33.] …
(10) … over all (sic) conditions warranted a finding of no
recognized environmental conditions due to ‘de minimis’
conditions, … do not present a threat to human health or the
environment and generally would not be the subject of an
enforcement action if brough (sic) to the attention of the
appropriate Government Agencies…”
39. Further, his conclusions that: (1) road access would remediate any disparity in value, and (2)
the decades of usage of the land by Appellants was a relevant factor, were not plainly wrong
and ought not to be set aside on appeal.
Ground 6
40. This ground alleges that the trial judge was wrong to affirm the partition as suggested by
Thompson J 2006 since this suggested partition had been set aside by the Court of Appeal and
affirmed by the Privy Council.
41. This ground is without merit.
42. Both the Court of Appeal and the Privy Council had decided to set aside the suggested
partition of Thompson J on the ground that it was premature. This was because the decision
on a partition should only have been made after a proper consideration of all the
circumstances.
43. Subsequent to the decision of the Court of Appeal and the Privy Council, the parties proceeded
to advance the partition action. They both made tactical decisions on this partition application,
(a) to insist on a partition rather than a sale; (b) to present competing affidavits; (c) to have no
cross examination.
9

44. As such, the trial judge was hamstrung as to his consideration of the facts. He therefore
exercised his discretion to partition the land as had been suggested by Thompson J since 2006
and gave his reasons for this.
45. I find no fault in the exercise of his discretion.
46. In oral submissions, we addressed the question of whether we should remit the matter to the
trial court for a more extensive consideration of the evidence.
47. I am of the humble view that it would be an abuse of process to remit this matter back to the
trial court for reconsideration. This would allow the Appellants a second bite of the cherry, in
the face of the facts that they made tactical decisions (a) to decide on the partition solely on
affidavit evidence (b) without cross-examination (c) in full knowledge of the unduly
protracted litigation spanning decades, over this disputed parcel of land.
CONCLUSION
48. I am of the view that this appeal should be dismissed.
49. Since the Respondent is clearly the successful party on this appeal, I am of the view that it
should have its costs of the appeal.
50. I therefore would order that the Appellants are to pay the Respondent’s costs of this appeal to
be taxed by the Registrar in default of agreement.
_______________________________________
The Honourable Mr. Justice Smith, JA
51. I agree.
_______________________________________
The Honourable Madam Justice Charles, JA
52. I also agree.
_______________________________________
The Honourable Madam Justice Fraser, JA
10

11
judges:
ments - Court of Appeal of the Commonwealth of The Bahamas 

 
 

 
 
 

 
 
 
 Return to Main Page
summary: Civil Appeal – Partition of land — Consent to partition — Affidavit evidence — No cross-examination — Weight of Evidence – Appellate interference with exercise of discretion – Valuation of parcels — Use and enjoyment of land

title: Denard Davis v. Director of Public Prosecutions
text:
COMMONWEALTH OF THE BAHAMAS
IN THE COURT OF APPEAL
SCCrApp No. 56 of 2024
B E T W E E N
DENARD DAVIS
Applicant
AND
DIRECTOR OF PUBLIC PROSECUTIONS
Respondent
BEFORE: The Honourable Mr. Justice Evans, P
The Honourable Mr. Justice Smith, JA
The Honourable Mr. Justice Kokaram, JA
APPEARANCES: Mr. Denard Davis, Pro Se
Ms. Shaneka Carey, Counsel for the Respondent
DATES: 30 March 2026; 17 April 2026
*******************************************************
Criminal Appeal – Application to reopen a concluded appeal – Jurisdiction of the court to reopen
a concluded appeal – Exceptional circumstances – Residual jurisdiction - Real injustice - Material
irregularity in the course of the trial – Safety of the verdict - Interests of justice – Finality – Articles
28, 104 and 105 of the Constitution
In September 2016, the Applicant was tried and convicted of murder and attempted murder,
receiving sentences of thirty-five (35) and eighteen (18) years respectively. He appealed the
conviction and sentence, which was dismissed in August 2018. His subsequent application for
leave to appeal to the Privy Council was refused in November 2022. A subsequent application to
this Court (differently constituted) seeking to reopen his appeal was likewise dismissed.
In February 2024, the Applicant applied for constitutional relief, claiming his right to a fair trial
was breached due to incompetent representation by his privately retained counsel. His application
for constitutional redress was made over five years after his initial appeal was dismissed. His
subsequent appeal against that dismissal was dismissed on the 27 June 2024. He then made the
current application seeking to reopen that appeal. We heard that application and dismissed the
same with written reasons to follow.
Held: The application is dismissed.
In our view, there are no exceptional circumstances which warrant this Court exercising its
jurisdiction to reopen the Applicant’s appeal.

The Applicant has made use of his right of constitutional redress pursuant to Article 28 (1) of the
Constitution, as well as his right to an appeal to this Court pursuant to Article 104 (1). He still has
available to him the benefit of Article 104 (2).
We are mindful of the fact that as noted earlier, the Applicant has had his matter considered
previously by the Privy Council, who determined that, “there was no risk that a miscarriage of
justice had occurred in his case”. In our view, if the Applicant has any new evidence of substance
which could potentially change that position, he ought to avail himself of his Article 104 (2) right
which is still available to him.
Daniel Coakley v. R SCCrApp. No. 15 of 2017; applied.
Edney L. Burrows Jr v R; Thaddeus Williams Jr. v R SCCrApp. Nos 12 and 13 of 2021; applied.
R v Gohil; R v Preko [2018] EWCA Crim 140; considered.
R v Henry [2018] 5 LRC 546; considered.
REASONS FOR DECISION
Delivered by the Honourable Mr. Justice Evans, P:
Introduction
1. This is an application by the Applicant to reopen a concluded appeal. It should be noted,
for context, that the Applicant has had two appeals before this Court and the current
application relates to the second appeal. The matters, however, are interconnected and the
factual matrix relative to how those progressed are of significance.
Background Facts
2. On 3 November 2010, Sheria Curry and Shanko Smith were outside in their yard in the
Fox Hill area when a Hyundai SUV drove up and shots were fired into the yard hitting
them. Ms. Curry was killed and Mr. Smith was injured. Witnesses to the tragedy identified
the shooters, but no identification was made of the driver.
3. The evidence against the Applicant was contained in a statement he gave to the police
under caution. In his statement to the police, the Applicant admitted that he drove the
vehicle in the drive by shooting, but stated that he did not know what his co-accused were
planning. He was charged along with two (2) others for the murder and attempted murder.
4. The evidence from the eyewitnesses contradicted the evidence of the Applicant’s version
of events, and suggested that the Applicant, as the driver of the vehicle, reduced the speed
of the car to facilitate the shooting by the vehicle’s passengers. The evidence of the
eyewitnesses was that, the vehicle drove past slowly, and while driving slowly, the
2

passengers pulled down the windows and shot at the victims, then pulled off and stopped
again, and further shots were fired.
5. After trial, the Applicant was found guilty of both charges, and sentenced to thirty-five (35)
years on the murder and eighteen (18) years on the attempted murder. The sentences were
to run concurrently. The Applicant appealed his conviction and sentence. On 7 August
2018, the Court dismissed his appeal and affirmed the conviction and sentence. The
Applicant then sought leave to appeal to the Privy Council. On 9 November 2022, the Privy
Council refused leave, on the basis that there was no risk that a miscarriage of justice had
occurred in his case.
6. The Applicant then made an application for constitutional redress by a letter dated 5
February 2024 to the Supreme Court. It claimed that his Article 20 right to a fair trial was
infringed by the incompetent representation of his attorney at his trial, and at his appeal to
the Court of Appeal. He also claimed redress for breach of his Article 17 right, but that
claim was abandoned at the hearing before Hilton J (as he then was). After a hearing before
Hilton J on 21 February 2024, at which the Applicant appeared on his own behalf, Hilton
J dismissed the application with reasons to follow. Reasons were given in a written ruling
delivered on 27 March 2024.
7. The Applicant then appealed to this Court against the decision of Hilton J. In that appeal,
he alleged that he did not receive a fair trial under Article 20. He said that, the incompetence
of his counsel (whom he and his family privately retained and not one provided by the
Crown) produced an unfair trial, and that this issue raised a constitutional point on the
fairness of the trial. The act and omission of counsel he claimed, had an impact on the
fairness of the trial.
8. In particular, the Applicant said that his counsel was instructed to challenge the statement
and record of interview because he was beaten by the police to give the statement, and the
record of the interview. He alleged that a doctor’s report spoke to bruises and pain and
medication was prescribed. He further claimed that, notwithstanding this evidence, his
attorney did not carry out his instructions to challenge the admissibility of the statement
and record of interview on those grounds. That omission, he asserted, constituted a
miscarriage of justice, and that the failure deprived him of an acquittal, as there was no
other evidence establishing that he was the driver of the vehicle from which the shots
killing Curry and injuring Smith were fired.
9. That appeal (SCCrApp. No. 56 of 2024 dated the 27 June 2024) was dismissed. Subsequent
to that, the Applicant made an application to reopen his appeal against conviction which
was dismissed on the 26 November 2024. By the current application, the Applicant sought
to reopen the appeal relative to his constitutional motion. We heard the application on the
30 March 2026 and dismissed the same, and promised to provide the Applicant with written
reasons for our decision. This we do now.
3

Discussion
10. The legal principles relative to an application for the reopening of an appeal are pretty well
settled. In the case of Daniel Coakley v. R SCCrApp. No. 15 of 2017, Sir Michael Barnett,
P observed as follows:
“The jurisdiction of a court to reopen a concluded appeal in a
criminal matter has been the subject of consideration by this
Court in a number of recent decisions: see Omar Archer v
Commissioner of Police MCCrApp. No. 140 of 2017. Though it
should only be exercised in exceptional circumstances, it is not
disputed that the jurisdiction exists. I refer to two cases which
were referred to in the Omar Archer decision.
In R v Gohil; R v Preko [2018] EWCA Crim 140 the facts of the
case as extracted from the head note are that:
‘The defendants in two separate cases were convicted of,
or pleaded guilty to, offences of fraud and money
laundering. In the first case the Court of Appeal dismissed
the defendant’s renewed application for leave to appeal
against conviction. In the second case the Court of Appeal
dismissed the defendant’s appeal against conviction. Both
defendants later applied to reopen the final determinations
of the Court of Appeal on the grounds that the Court of
Appeal had been misled by the Crown’s failure to disclose
relevant material.’
The English Court of Appeal said:
‘110. On the footing upon which R v Yasain was decided,
namely that there is no difference between the jurisdiction
of the Civil Division and that of the CACD to reopen
previous final determinations, it can safely be said that of
the CACD will not reopen a final determination of any
appeal unless:
(i) It is necessary to do so in order to avoid real injustice;
(ii) The circumstances are exceptional and make it
appropriate to reopen the appeal; and
(iii) There is no alternative effective remedy.’
In the case of R v Henry [2018] 5 LRC 546, Henry was convicted
and sentenced for the offence of causing dangerous harm. He
appealed to the Court of Appeal which, by oral judgment eight
4

days following the hearing of the appeal, dismissed Henry’s
appeal and confirmed his conviction and sentence. At that time
the court indicated that its reasons would follow. When the
court’s written judgment was produced the court allowed the
appeal and quashed Henry’s conviction and sentence with no
reference to the earlier oral judgment which dismissed the
appeal. The DPP sought to appeal the written judgment on the
basis that the court became functus officio from the date of the
oral judgment.
The DPP’s appeal to the Caribbean Court of Justice (CCJ) was
allowed on the basis of errors by the Court of Appeal but in the
course of delivering its judgment the CCJ accepted that:
‘[17] …It is thus settled law that a court has an inherent
power to even reopen a criminal appeal to ensure that
justice is done...’
Of course, there can be no fixed circumstances where this
jurisdiction can be exercised, save that it must be only in
exceptional circumstances. This is particularly so in criminal
cases where finality plays an important role. This point was
made in Edney L. Burrows Jr v R; Thaddeus Williams Jr. v R
SCCrApp. Nos 12 and 13 of 2021 where the Court said:
‘22. The jurisprudence does make it clear that in criminal
matters, and the compelling need for finality, the exercise
of this jurisdiction must be restricted to exceptional
circumstances’.”
11. In our view, there are no exceptional circumstances which warrant this Court exercising its
jurisdiction to reopen the Applicant’s appeal. Article 28 of the Constitution provides that:
“28. (1) If any person alleges that any of the provisions of
Articles 16 to 27 (inclusive) of this Constitution has been, is
being or is likely to be contravened in relation to him then,
without prejudice to any other action with respect to the same
matter which is lawfully available, that person may apply to the
Supreme Court for redress.”
12. The Applicant has made use of his right to Article 28 (1) of the Constitution as well as his
right to an appeal to this Court pursuant to Article 104 (1). He still has available to him the
benefit of Article 104 (2). Article 104 provides as follows:
5

“104. (1)An appeal to the Court of Appeal shall lie as of right
from the final decisions of the Supreme Court given in exercise
of the jurisdiction conferred on the Supreme Court by Article
28 of this Constitution (which relates to the enforcement of
fundamental rights and freedoms).
(2) An appeal shall lie as of right to the Judicial Committee of
Her Majesty’s Privy Council or to such other court as may be
prescribed by Parliament under Article 105(3) of this
Constitution from any decision given by the Court of Appeal in
any such case.”
13. We are mindful of the fact that as noted earlier, the Applicant has had his matter considered
previously by the Privy Council, who determined that “there was no risk that a
miscarriage of justice had occurred in his case.” In our view, if the Applicant has any
new evidence of substance which could potentially change that position, he ought to avail
himself of his Article 104 (2) right which is still available to him. Parliament not having
prescribed any other Court pursuant to Article 105 (3), the Applicant is able to seek redress
from His Majesty’s Privy Council.
Disposition
14. It was for these reasons that we dismissed the Applicant’s application to reopen his appeal.
.
________________________________________
The Honorable Mr. Justice Evans, P
________________________________________
The Honourable Mr. Justice Smith, JA
________________________________________
The Honourable Mr. Justice Kokaram, JA
6
judges:
ments - Court of Appeal of the Commonwealth of The Bahamas
